title: Type agent_loop.py Anthropic/OpenAI SDK calls with proper SDK types
analysis:
agent_loop.py has 7 pyright warnings in _call_openai (lines 255-265, 273) plus 2 import-resolution errors (lines 198, 223 — lazy imports of anthropic/openai that pyright can't resolve). The 7 warnings break down as: 5 reportAttributeAccessIssue from hasattr-based content block dispatch (accessing .type, .text, .id, .name, .input on dict[Unknown, Unknown] because pyright sees the content list as list[dict] and hasattr doesn't narrow types), 1 reportArgumentType from appending a dict with list values to a list typed as list[dict[str, str]], and 1 more reportAttributeAccessIssue. Root cause: method signatures use bare `list[dict]` making pyright infer dict values as Unknown, and hasattr checks don't enable type narrowing.

Three fixes needed: (1) Replace hasattr dispatch in _call_openai with isinstance using _TextBlock/_ToolUseBlock (the actual runtime types — produced by _OpenAIResponseAdapter). (2) Fix tools=None on line 273 using conditional kwargs dict (OpenAI SDK uses NOT_GIVEN sentinel internally; None is type-invalid). (3) Add TYPE_CHECKING imports for SDK TypedDicts and properly annotate types at SDK call boundaries.

Key insight: In the OpenAI code path, content blocks are ALWAYS _TextBlock/_ToolUseBlock from _OpenAIResponseAdapter (not Anthropic SDK TextBlock/ToolUseBlock), because provider='openai' is fixed for the entire AgentLoop session. So isinstance checks should target the internal adapter types. The pyproject.toml has pyright severity downgrades (reportArgumentType/reportAttributeAccessIssue = 'warning') with a comment 'tracked in task #50' — these revert to default ('error') once fixed.

All 11 existing tests pass (they mock _call_llm, so _call_openai has zero direct test coverage). New tests added before implementation.

steps.description: Write test_openai_content_block_dispatch: Import _TextBlock, _ToolUseBlock from agent_loop. Build a messages list simulating a multi-turn conversation: (a) initial user message with string content, (b) assistant message with content=[_TextBlock('thinking'), _ToolUseBlock(id='tc1', name='my_tool', input={'x': 1})], (c) user message with tool_result dicts. Mock AsyncOpenAI.chat.completions.create to capture the openai_messages arg. Call agent._call_openai(messages, tool_schemas). Assert the converted openai_messages contain: system message, user text, assistant text from _TextBlock, assistant tool_calls from _ToolUseBlock, and tool result. Test should pass with current hasattr code (establishes baseline coverage before refactoring).

In _call_openai lines 255-268, replace `hasattr(block, 'type') and block.type == 'text'` with `isinstance(block, _TextBlock)` and `hasattr(block, 'type') and block.type == 'tool_use'` with `isinstance(block, _ToolUseBlock)`. No new imports needed — both types are defined in the same file. This resolves 5 reportAttributeAccessIssue warnings by enabling pyright type narrowing: after isinstance(_TextBlock), pyright knows block.text exists; after isinstance(_ToolUseBlock), pyright knows block.id/name/input exist.

Write test_openai_tools_omitted_when_empty: Create an AgentLoop with provider='openai'. Mock AsyncOpenAI and capture kwargs passed to chat.completions.create(). Call agent._call_openai with tool_schemas=[]. Assert that 'tools' key is NOT in captured kwargs. This test FAILS with current code (line 273 passes tools=None when openai_tools is empty).

Fix line 270-274: Replace direct create() call with conditional kwargs. Build `create_kwargs: dict[str, Any] = {'model': self.config.agent_llm_model, 'messages': openai_messages}`, then `if openai_tools: create_kwargs['tools'] = openai_tools`, then `response = await client.chat.completions.create(**create_kwargs)`. This avoids passing tools=None and eliminates the type error.

Write test_to_anthropic_schema_returns_tool_param_shape: Create a ToolDefinition, call to_anthropic_schema(), and assert the returned dict has exactly the keys ToolParam requires: 'name' (str), 'input_schema' (dict), 'description' (str). Validates structural compatibility. Test passes immediately (locks in contract before type annotation change).

Add TYPE_CHECKING imports and type annotations: (1) Add `from typing import TYPE_CHECKING` and guard block importing ToolParam from anthropic.types, and ChatCompletionMessageParam/ChatCompletionToolParam from openai.types.chat. (2) Change to_anthropic_schema() return type from `dict` to `ToolParam`. (3) Change _call_llm, _call_openai, _call_claude_cli message/tool_schemas params from `list[dict]` to `list[dict[str, Any]]`. (4) Annotate openai_messages and openai_tools local variables in _call_openai with the OpenAI SDK TypedDicts. (5) Change run() local `messages: list[dict]` to `list[dict[str, Any]]`. All annotations use string form via `from __future__ import annotations` already at line 3. Run tests to verify no regressions.

Update pyproject.toml pyright config: Remove the comment '# agent_loop.py SDK calls use untyped dicts — tracked in task #50' and delete the two suppression lines `reportArgumentType = "warning"` and `reportAttributeAccessIssue = "warning"`. These revert to basic-mode defaults (error). Run pyright on agent_loop.py to verify the targeted warnings are resolved. Run full test suite to confirm no regressions.

design_decisions.decision: Use local _TextBlock/_ToolUseBlock for isinstance, not Anthropic SDK TextBlock/ToolUseBlock
design_decisions.rationale: In _call_openai, content blocks are always _TextBlock/_ToolUseBlock from _OpenAIResponseAdapter (since provider='openai' is fixed for the entire AgentLoop session). Anthropic SDK types would never match at runtime. Using adapter types gives correct isinstance narrowing and resolves pyright warnings through the actual runtime types.

design_decisions.decision: Conditional kwargs dict for OpenAI tools parameter instead of NOT_GIVEN sentinel
design_decisions.rationale: OpenAI SDK uses NOT_GIVEN as default for tools — passing None is type-invalid. Conditional kwargs dict (`if openai_tools: create_kwargs['tools'] = openai_tools`) avoids importing NOT_GIVEN, is more readable, and is idiomatic Python for optional SDK parameters.

design_decisions.decision: Use list[dict[str, Any]] for internal message format, SDK TypedDicts only at SDK boundaries
design_decisions.rationale: The internal message list mixes string content, adapter content blocks, and tool-result dicts — a hybrid format that doesn't cleanly map to any SDK's MessageParam. dict[str, Any] resolves pyright's Unknown-attribute issues. Properly typed locals at SDK boundaries (list[ChatCompletionMessageParam]) enforce safety where it matters.

design_decisions.decision: Remove pyright severity downgrades from pyproject.toml after fixing
design_decisions.rationale: Lines 35-37 of pyproject.toml downgrade reportArgumentType and reportAttributeAccessIssue to 'warning' with a comment tracking task #50. Once root causes are fixed, reverting to basic-mode defaults (error) catches future regressions.

design_decisions.decision: TYPE_CHECKING guard for SDK TypedDict imports only
design_decisions.rationale: ToolParam, ChatCompletionMessageParam, ChatCompletionToolParam are only used in annotations. With `from __future__ import annotations` (already present), annotations are strings at runtime. Guarding behind TYPE_CHECKING avoids runtime import cost. No new runtime imports needed — _TextBlock/_ToolUseBlock are already in the same file.

reuse.how: Used in isinstance() checks for content block dispatch in _call_openai. These are the actual runtime types of blocks in the OpenAI/CLI paths. Also imported in tests to construct realistic message content.

reuse.what: Existing test infrastructure (FakeToolUse, FakeText, FakeResponse, FakeUsage, _make_config)
reuse.how: Reuse _make_config with agent_llm_provider='openai' for new OpenAI-specific tests. FakeResponse/FakeUsage for mock response construction.
Understanding that it produces _TextBlock/_ToolUseBlock (not SDK types) informs the isinstance design — only adapter types need to be checked in the OpenAI path.
Enables TYPE_CHECKING-only imports for SDK types used in annotations — annotations become strings and are never evaluated at runtime.
